message.content:
You are an expert chess coach and trainer (FIDE 2500+) writing ONE markdown file for a comprehensive "1500 to 2800 FIDE" study curriculum. Write GitHub-flavored Markdown, accurate, concrete and coach-like. Target 1000 to 1800 words.

CRITICAL — CHESS ACCURACY: Use ONLY legal positions and SOUND tactics/lines. Strongly prefer famous, textbook-verified positions and keep example positions MINIMAL (few pieces) so they are easy to verify. Use standard algebraic notation. Make sure every stated mate/winning line actually works and that each ASCII board EXACTLY matches its FEN. Double-check before saving.

REQUIRED STRUCTURE (use these exact section headings in order):
1. A H1 title "# <Concept Name>", then a one-line blockquote definition, then two lines: "**Rating focus:** <band>" and "**Prerequisites:** <relative links or 'none'>".
2. "## The idea" — plain-English explanation.
3. "## Why it matters" — practical value at the board.
4. "## Diagrams" — at least TWO board diagrams. For EACH diagram use THIS EXACT ASCII format inside a fenced code block: 8 rows, ranks 8 (top) down to 1 (bottom); UPPERCASE letters = White pieces (K Q R B N P), lowercase = black pieces (k q r b n p), '.' = empty square; prefix each row with its rank digit and a space; final line is "  a b c d e f g h". Immediately after the code block add: an italic caption, a "**FEN:** <exact fen>" line, a side-to-move note, and a Lichess link of the form https://lichess.org/analysis/<FEN with every space replaced by an underscore>. Then explain the key moves in algebraic notation.
5. "## Patterns and sub-types" — bullet the main variations and related motifs.
6. "## How to spot it" — a Mermaid diagram in a fenced code block whose info string is "mermaid" and whose first content line is "flowchart TD"; a recognition/decision tree of 5 to 9 nodes. Node text in square brackets, arrows use -->, edge labels use |label|. Keep labels short and avoid parentheses, quotes, colons, slashes or other characters that break Mermaid.
7. "## Worked examples" — at least two short examples, each with an ASCII diagram (same format), FEN, and a clear solution.
8. "## Common mistakes" — bullets.
9. "## Train it" — concrete drills, rep counts, and where to practice (Lichess puzzle themes, named books, etc.).
10. "## Exercises" — 3 to 5 puzzles, each a FEN + side to move + the task; put all answers inside one HTML block: <details><summary>Show answers</summary> ... </details>.
11. "## Related" — relative markdown links to sibling files like [Zugzwang](zugzwang.md), plus double-bracket concept tags like [[zugzwang]].

Write the file, then SAVE it with the Write tool to the EXACT absolute path provided. After saving, reply with ONE short line: the path and the word count.

TOPIC FOR THIS FILE: Opposite-Colored Bishop Endgames
GUIDANCE / POSITIONS TO USE: Why two extra pawns can still draw with opposite bishops; the defender blockades on its color; in the middlegame the attacker often wins. Minimal diagrams.
EXACT SAVE PATH (use Write to this absolute path): C:/GrandMaster/05-Endgames/opposite-colored-bishops.md

Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- contribute:contribute: This skill should be used when the user asks to "contribute to open source", "find an issue to work on", "analyze a repo", "submit a pull request", "review PR feedback", "triage an issue", "sync my fork", "create a release", "clean up contribution branches", "debug CI failures", or mentions open-source contribution workflows. Provides a full-lifecycle open-source contribution system with 12 phases.

Combines a deliberate design process (brainstorm a token system, critique it, commit the palette, then build) with principles-over-prescriptions taste guidance, render-verified mechanics, and a copy-writing section — so the output is intentional, polished, and never reads as a template.

- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.

Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.

- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.

Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

The core fortress: opposite-colored bishops where the defender blockades passed pawns on squares of their bishop's color. Let me write the file with carefully checked positions.

input.content:
# Opposite-Colored Bishop Endgames

> Endgames where each side has one bishop and the two bishops travel on squares of different colors, so they can never trade or directly oppose each other.

**Rating focus:** 1700–2400
**Prerequisites:** [Bishop vs Bishop Basics](bishop-endgames.md), [The Opposition](the-opposition.md)

## The idea

When the bishops run on opposite colors, neither can attack or defend the squares the other controls. The board effectively splits into two non-overlapping worlds. In a pure pawn-up endgame this is the most drawish material in chess: the famous rule of thumb is that **even two extra pawns can fail to win** if the defender can erect a blockade on squares of his own bishop's color. The defending bishop sits in front of the passed pawns on its color, the attacking king and bishop simply cannot evict it, and a fortress arises.

The reverse is true with queens, rooks, or many pieces on the board. In the **middlegame**, opposite bishops favor the *attacker*, because the attacking bishop dominates a color complex (often around the enemy king) that the defender's bishop can never contest. Same geometry, opposite verdict.

## Why it matters

Knowing the blockade saves and wins half-points constantly. As the *stronger* side you must learn the drawing tendency so you do not liquidate into a dead-drawn opposite-bishop ending a pawn or two up. As the *weaker* side you steer toward it deliberately: trading the right pieces into opposite bishops is one of the most reliable ways to rescue a lost position. And in the middlegame you flip the dial — keep heavy pieces on and aim your bishop at the squares your opponent's bishop cannot guard.

## Diagrams

```
8 . . . . . . . .
7 . . . . . . . .
6 . . k . . . . .
5 . p p . . . . .
4 . P P . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . . . .
1 . K B . b . . .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*White is two pawns up but the dark-squared bishop blockades on c5/b6 dark squares — dead draw.*
**FEN:** 8/8/2k5/1pp5/1PP5/8/8/1KB1b3 w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/8/8/2k5/1pp5/1PP5/8/8/1KB1b3_w_-_-_0_1)

The white pawns are fixed on light squares (b4, c4) but they cannot advance because c5 and b5 are covered. White's light bishop guards light squares; Black's bishop on e1 patrols the a5–e1 dark diagonal and stops any breakthrough. White can never make progress: a textbook **fortress**.

```
8 . . . . . . . k
7 . . . . . . p p
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . B P P
2 . . . . . . . K
1 . . . . . . b .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*Middlegame logic in miniature — but here, pawns-only, this too is a draw; the win comes when heavy pieces stay on.*
**FEN:** 7k/6pp/8/8/8/5BPP/7K/6b1 w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/7k/6pp/8/8/8/5BPP/7K/6b1_w_-_-_0_1)

With only bishops, this is trivially drawn. Add a pair of rooks or queens and White's light-squared bishop, aiming at h7 and the light squares around the black king, would give a powerful attack that the dark-squared bishop cannot parry — the middlegame attacking principle.

## Patterns and sub-types

- **Two connected passers, both on the bishop's color:** if the defender blockades the front square, the pawns are frozen. Often a draw even two pawns up.
- **Two connected passers split by one file the defender's bishop controls:** the defender plants his bishop and king to stop one, blockades the other.
- **The "wrong" rook pawn idea extended:** a passed pawn whose queening square is the *opposite* color to the attacker's bishop can be a fortress with the lone defending king.
- **Distant passed pawns (two widely separated files):** these usually WIN, because the defender's single bishop and king cannot cover both wings — the key winning structure.
- **Middlegame with heavy pieces:** opposite bishops amplify an attack; the side attacking has an "extra piece" on the relevant color.

## How to spot it

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[Opposite-colored bishops] --> B{Heavy pieces still on}
  B -->|Yes| C[Attacker is favored aim bishop at king color]
  B -->|No, pawn ending| D{Passers connected or close}
  D -->|Connected| E[Likely draw blockade front square]
  D -->|Widely separated| F[Likely win two fronts]
  E --> G{Defender bishop controls block square}
  G -->|Yes| H[Fortress draw]
  G -->|No| I[Push and probe for win]
```

## Worked examples

```
8 . . . . . . . .
7 . . . . . . . .
6 . . . . . k . .
5 . . . . . P . .
4 . . . . . K . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . b . . . . . .
1 . . . B . . . .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*White to move; one pawn up — can White make progress?*
**FEN:** 8/8/5k2/5P2/5K2/8/1b6/3B4 w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/8/8/5k2/5P2/5K2/8/1b6/3B4_w_-_-_0_1)

Solution: This is a draw. The pawn's queening square f8 is dark, but Black simply keeps his king and bishop controlling the pawn's path. After 1.Kg4 Bc1 2.f5+? is not even possible (f5 is occupied); White cannot shield the pawn's advance from the black king because his light bishop never guards f6/f8. Black just shuffles the bishop on the a7–g1 diagonal and keeps his king near f6/f7. The single passer cannot promote: **draw**.

```
8 . . . . . . . .
7 . . . . . . . .
6 . . . k . . . .
5 . . . P . . P .
4 . . . K . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . b . . . .
1 . . B . . . . .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*White to move; two widely separated passers (d5 and g5).*
**FEN:** 8/8/3k4/3P2P1/3K4/8/3b4/2B5 w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/8/8/3k4/3P2P1/3K4/8/3b4/2B5_w_-_-_0_1)

Solution: This wins. The pawns are far apart, so the lone black bishop and king cannot stop both. White advances the g-pawn (1.g6) and uses the bishop to support one passer while the king escorts the other. The defender must commit to one wing; the second front decides. The rule: **separated passers beat opposite bishops, connected ones draw.**

## Common mistakes

- Trading into opposite bishops when *up material* and then being unable to win — count whether your passers are connected (drawish) or separated (winning).
- As the defender, blockading on the *wrong* color square so the attacking bishop can chase your piece away.
- Forgetting that with heavy pieces on, the same opposite bishops mean *attack*, not draw — passively defending instead of seeking counterplay.
- Pushing a connected pair so they sit on squares the defending bishop covers, freezing your own winning chances.
- Letting the defending king reach the blockade square; the bishop plus king together build the fortress.

## Train it

- Lichess puzzle themes: **"endgame"** + **"bishopEndgame"**; filter for opposite-bishop motifs. Do 20 reps focusing on blockade vs. breakthrough.
- Drill the two-connected-pawns fortress (first diagram) against the engine from both sides, 10 reps each side, until the draw is automatic.
- Drill the separated-passers win (second worked example), 10 reps as the stronger side.
- Books: Dvoretsky's *Endgame Manual* (chapter on opposite-colored bishops) and Müller & Lamprecht's *Fundamental Chess Endings*. Read and replay every example once, then reconstruct three from memory.

## Exercises

1. **FEN:** 8/8/2k5/1pp5/1PP5/8/8/1KB1b3 w - - 0 1 — White to move. Can White win the two extra pawns home, or is it a draw? State the verdict and why.
2. **FEN:** 8/8/3k4/3P2P1/3K4/8/3b4/2B5 w - - 0 1 — White to move. Draw or win? Give the plan.
3. **FEN:** 8/8/5k2/5P2/5K2/8/1b6/3B4 w - - 0 1 — White to move. Is the single passer enough to win?
4. **FEN:** 7k/6pp/8/8/8/5BPP/7K/6b1 w - - 0 1 — White to move. Result with only bishops, and how would it change with a pair of rooks added?

<details><summary>Show answers</summary>

1. **Draw.** The pawns are fixed on light squares (b4, c4) and the dark-squared bishop blockades c5/b5; White's light bishop can never contest those squares, so the passers are frozen forever — fortress.
2. **Win.** The passers on d5 and g5 are widely separated. Advance with 1.g6 and use king plus bishop to escort one pawn while the other ties down the defender. A lone bishop and king cannot cover both wings.
3. **No, draw.** A single passer with opposite bishops cannot be forced through when the defending king and bishop control its path; White's light bishop never guards f6/f8.
4. **Draw** with bishops only. Add a pair of rooks (or queens) and White is much better/winning: the light-squared bishop targets h7 and the light squares around Black's king, an attack the dark bishop cannot parry — opposite bishops favor the attacker in the middlegame.

</details>

## Related

- [Bishop vs Bishop Basics](bishop-endgames.md)
- [The Opposition](the-opposition.md)
- [Fortress](fortress.md)
- [Zugzwang](zugzwang.md)

[[fortress]] [[blockade]] [[opposite-colored-bishops]] [[passed-pawns]]
